engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: Parked a knight on e5 that nobody actually guarded
mistakes.explanation: The leap to e5 looks like a dream outpost, and at a glance the rook on a5 covers it along the fifth rank. Trace that rank square by square, though, and your own bishop on c5 is standing in the way — the rook never sees e5 at all. After 18.Nxe5 the knight is simply gone: …Rxe5 is illegal, and no other piece touches the square. White played 18.Ng5? instead and handed the piece back, but this was a whole knight left in the middle of the board. 17…h6 stops the h-pawn's creep, keeps every piece defended, and prepares …O-O with a winning position.

drillLinks.label: Loose pieces drop off — blunder-proofing discussion

takeaways.lesson: A defender only defends if its path is clear — X-ray sight doesn't count.
takeaways.detail: When you count a rook or bishop as a defender, trace its line square by square with your eyes. Your own pieces block it just as thoroughly as the opponent's do. Here one glance along the fifth rank finds the c5-bishop in the way.

takeaways.lesson: Before any knight jump, count attackers and defenders on the landing square.
takeaways.detail: Knights land deep in enemy territory, so make the count a ritual: how many of theirs hit the square, how many of mine genuinely defend it? If the answer is 1–0 against you, the outpost is a blunder, however central it looks.

mistakes.title: Reassigned the bodyguard and left h5 to the rook
mistakes.explanation: Your knight on h5 stood naked in the h1-rook's line — no pawn or piece defended it. What kept 24.Rxh5 off the board was pure tactics: with the other knight on e5, the capture runs into 24…Nxd3+! 25.Kd1 Nf2+ and …Nxe4 hitting the queen, winning everything back with the attack still raging. 23…Ng4 moved exactly that knight, so 24.Rxh5 would now just win a piece — the eval fell from +6.2 to +1.7 on the spot. The bot chose 24.Bg2? instead, and your 24…Nhf6 scrambled the knight to safety. The engine wanted 23…Ra3, piling onto d3 and daring White to take on h5.

drillLinks.label: Loose pieces drop off — blunder-proofing discussion

takeaways.lesson: Loose pieces drop off — keep a running list of your undefended pieces.
takeaways.detail: After every move of yours, name each piece that no pawn or piece of yours defends. The h5-knight had been on that list for three moves; a piece that stays on the list is a tactic waiting for the opponent to find.

takeaways.lesson: If a piece is only saved by a counter-tactic, the piece providing that tactic is on duty.
takeaways.detail: The e5-knight wasn't just active — it was the reason Rxh5 failed. Before moving any piece, ask what tactical jobs it is doing, not only what squares it defends. Jobs count as much as direct protection.

mistakes.title: Backed away from a pawn tension you were winning
mistakes.explanation: After 9.c3 the position held double tension — a5 against b4, d4 against c3 — and the captures worked in your favor: 9…axb4! wins a pawn by force, because 10.cxd4 is met by the zwischenzug 10…Nxd4! 11.Nexd4 exd4 and your extra b4-pawn stands tall. Instead 9…Nd7 un-developed a piece and handed White the choice of when and how to resolve everything: 10.b5! would have shoved the c6-knight to the rim before scooping up d4. The game's 10.cxd4 exd4 let you escape lightly, but the eval had already swung nearly four pawns.

drillLinks.label: When to push the pawns in front of the king — discussion

takeaways.lesson: When pawns stand in tension, calculate the captures before considering anything else.
takeaways.detail: Pawn tension is a countdown: whoever resolves it on their terms wins the structure. Work out every capture sequence concretely — here 9…axb4 was forcing and simply won a pawn. Only if the captures fail do quiet moves get a turn.

takeaways.lesson: Don't retreat a developed piece while the position is on fire.
takeaways.detail: 9…Nd7 moved a good knight backwards in the sharpest moment of the opening. When captures and threats are in the air, forcing moves come first; regrouping is for quiet positions.

mistakes.title: A one-move threat that abandoned the e5-pawn
mistakes.explanation: 6…Nb4 dreams of the fork on c2 — but c2 is covered by the queen on d1, so White can ignore the gesture entirely. Meanwhile the knight that jumped was the only defender of your e5-pawn: 7.Nxe5! wins a clean central pawn, and after …Nxc2+? 8.Qxc2 you'd lose a piece trying to justify the raid. White obliged with 7.a3? Nc6 8.b4 instead — no pawn lost, but you'd spent two tempi to put the knight back where it started. The engine's 6…Bb4+ 7.Bd2 Bxd2+ 8.Qxd2 trades into a comfortable, slightly better game.

takeaways.lesson: Before moving a piece, name out loud what it currently defends.
takeaways.detail: Every piece has a day job. The c6-knight's was guarding e5. Make it a pre-move ritual: what does this piece protect, and who takes over when it leaves? If the answer is 'nobody', the move needs a much better reason.

takeaways.lesson: A threat the opponent can safely ignore is not a threat.
takeaways.detail: Check the target's defenders before you 'attack' it: c2 was already covered by the queen, so Nb4 carried no real punch. One-move gestures that get shrugged off cost tempi — and tempi are the currency of the opening.

mistakes.title: Three checks from the finish line, you looked away
mistakes.explanation: With the White king stranded on d1 and every White piece a spectator, 36…Ra1+! forces mate on the spot: 37.Bc1 is the only reply, and then 37…Rxc1+! 38.Kxc1 Qa1# — rook sacrifice, queen delivers. Your 36…Ra2 kept a completely won position (you still mated fifteen moves later), but it gave the king ten extra moves of life and left the finish to chance. Winning positions are exactly when calculation should get sharper, not looser: every check deserved one look here.

takeaways.lesson: When your attack looks decisive, calculate every check before anything else.
takeaways.detail: Checks are the most forcing moves on the board — the opponent's replies are countable on one hand. In won positions, run through each check and each capture to the end; if one of them mates, nothing else on the board matters.

takeaways.lesson: Drill short mates so the patterns fire at the board.
takeaways.detail: Ra1+, Rxc1+, Qa1# is a stock back-rank clearance pattern. Ten minutes of mate-in-2 and mate-in-3 puzzles a day makes these finishes automatic — you stop searching for them and start seeing them.

highlights.note: The queenside file-opening you built with …a5 and …axb4 was the right plan at the right moment — the a-file pressure and the passed b-pawn that won this game started here.
Same square as the move-17 blunder, opposite result: this time …Ne5 came with tempo — hitting the f3-bishop and the d3-pawn — and nothing hung. Only 11% of your level centralizes here.
A quiet regrouping in a winning attack — …Bd7 heading for a4 to harass the defenders. Just 7% of players at your level find this move; you outplayed your band here.

openingReport.note: Stockfish's 1.Nc3 (Van Geet) leaves book almost immediately and 1…Nf6 is a fine reply. The first real opening slip came at 6…Nb4 (57.9% → 34.2%): the knight jumped to a square White could kick, when 6…Bb4+ developed the bishop with check instead.
